# Week 1: Introduction to Quantum Computing

## Video 4

### Postulates of Quantum Mechanics - I

- [Music] hi in the second segment we will basically discuss physical properties and the mathematical structure of the quantum bit right so let's quickly recall from the first module that the quantum bit or qubit for short is basically represented by a vector in a vector space right so this is what we call a ket vector right which can be written as an arbitrary complex linear combination of two ket vectors this is the ket 0 and this is the ket 1 which correspond to the classical bit values 0 and 1. right we already saw this in the first segment and we also said that can not only take the value 0 or 1 but can also exist in some linear combination of 0 and 1. the quantum bit 0 and the quantum bit 1 are denoted by these ket vectors and alpha and beta are complex numbers that have to satisfy mod alpha squared plus mod beta squared is 1 right so this is the succinct mathematical description of the qubit what we will try to do in today's lecture is to understand the physical properties of this object better and in the process also understand the mathematical structure of the qubit space right so in particular what we will aim to do in this lecture is to address the following questions right what is the underlying physical description of a qubit right and the answer comes to us in the form of what we will call two level quantum systems right so the aim of this lecture will be to understand these two level quantum systems in particular we would like to understand how do we represent physical properties of such quantum systems and thirdly how do such quantum systems evolve with time equally well we can ask how do we transform qubit states okay so basically we need to understand the underlying physical properties of quantum systems and the associated mathematical structure and that will help us understand how to compute with quantum bits right and the answer to these three questions will come in the form of the so-called postulates of quantum mechanics i would just like to add a disclaimer here that in this for the purposes of this course we will focus exclusively on finite dimensional quantum systems in particular two level systems rather we will stick to a matrix formulation of quantum theory ok so the first thing we will discuss now is the first postulate so postulate one has to do with the description of the states of a quantum system so the state of a quantum system is described or represented by a unit vector in a complex inner product space more generally a hilbert space right so uh again i should remark here that we will not get into the formal definition of a hilbert space here suffice it to say that for finite dimensional systems which is what we are concerned with a complex linear vector space with an inner product is essentially a hilbert space right so we may use these terms interchangeably since we are dealing only with finite dimensional hilbert spaces okay so let's try to now dwell on this postulate a bit and understand this better so what it means is the state of a system unlike in classical mechanics or for classical systems where the state of a system is described by the position and momentum of a given particle or so collection of particles then it is the set of all positions and momenta or velocities of these particles that constitutes a description of the state of the system right unlike that scenario which is the classical scenario in the quantum case the state is described by a vector in a vector space so that's the first key departure from classical systems so the state of a system is represented so right so this is what we get psi rather that is what we will call this and this belongs to some complex linear vector space which i will call h h is the hilbert space or the complex linear vector space associated with the system so let us do a quick recap of uh two things first the notation and secondly the idea of a vector space so let's quickly recap what we need to know about

# Week 1: Introduction to Quantum Computing

## Video 4

### Postulates of Quantum Mechanics - I

linear vector spaces right so it is basically a set of vectors which we will again represent by this ket right so v is some collection of vectors right such that there are two properties which we will often develop on here if lambda is a complex number so in particular i want to focus on complex linear vector spaces right so if lambda is a complex number here c denotes the field of complex numbers so if lambda is any complex number then lambda so i should say for all lambda lambda this is the belongs to sine right for all lambda belonging to c lambda times v is also an element of this vector space secondly if v 1 and v 2 are two vectors that belong to the vector space then any complex linear combination of the form alpha v1 plus beta v2 also belongs to the vector space so these are two important properties that we should keep in mind before we discuss quantum systems further the second remark that i want to make is about notation so when i say vector in a vector space many of you may recall the real three-dimensional space and the vectors that we are known used to dealing with in real three dimensional spaces indeed the real three dimensional space also forms a vector space but in this course we will be dealing with more abstract vectors and so we need to have some notation to denote these vectors and that is where this bracket notation comes into play originally due to the physicist dirac right so we will denote any vector in a vector space by putting this ket around it so this is called ket v right with a right angle bracket right so this denotes any arbitrary vector in a vector space so this is the notation we will use for vector in a vector space and as we see in the first postulate this in the quantum setting becomes a quantum state right state of a quantum system okay secondly while this is what we call the ket vector i should also introduce here the notion of a dual or a bra vector so this would be called bravi this is the dual of the ket and the dual simply means in a matrix representation and i will just tell you what is the matrix representation we are going to use the matrix representation the dual simply means um this is the conjugate transpose or the complex conjugate transpose of ketv right which means that if i represent this ket in some matrix form then so if we take both the complex conjugate and the transpose of this ketv then we end up with the dual of the ket which is called the bra vector right and both of these objects become very important for us in describing properties of quantum systems so let me then quickly also go into the matrix notation right away so the third aspect is the matrix representation so if kept v belongs to some d dimensional complex linear vector space then we can represent get v as a d-dimensional column vector i will tell you what a column vector is right now with complex entries so v has d components because it lives in a d dimensional space right so these d components of v can be put into what's called a column vector so the column vector essentially is a d cross one matrix right it has just one column it's a one column matrix so that's why it's called a column vector right and the entries of this look like v 1 v 2 etc v sub d where v 1 v 2 all the way up to v d belong to the set of the field of complex numbers right and so correspondingly the bra vector which will be represented like this then becomes the remember it's a complex conjugate transpose so i take this v1 v2 etc vd i do the complex conjugate and i do the transpose when i do the transpose i end up with a row vector right so the bra vector becomes a row has a row form whereas the ket vector has a column form and the bra vector essentially now has entries v1 star v2 star etc vd star and this is a one cross d matrix right okay or the final thing we need to note from uh the language of linear vector spaces is the notion of inner product okay then i can

### Postulates of Quantum Mechanics - I

define the inner product between two vectors let's say i want to take the inner product between vectors v and w this can be represented as so as this which is basically a bra times a ket right and remember that the bra vector is a row vector the kept vector is a column vector so when i and this can simply be represented as the matrix multiplication of the bra and the kit so this simply becomes v 1 star v 2 star etc v d star times let's say the elements of w are w 1 w 2 etc w d then this simply becomes sum over i v i star wi so this is in general a complex number right this is a scalar what we to contrast it from vector we call it a scalar which is basically a complex number so this is what i mean by the inner product between two vectors i basically take the conjugate transpose of the vector and i multiply the two right this is the inner product between v and w the inner product between w and v will be written the other way around with the bra w and the ket v okay the next the last thing is actually uh that i want to talk about is the outer product as well right so once we have the vector and its dual we can construct two kinds of products one is the inner product which takes a bra with a ket which acts a bra with a ket and leads us to a number or a scalar the second is to do a so this is a bracket right the second thing we can do is a ket bra right which means i can write something like this which is a cat v followed by a bra w what kind of object is this so if i just go by dimensions then you know that the ket is a one cross d object the bra is ah i'm sorry this is a d cross one object right and the bra is a one cross d object so what we end up with is basically by matrix multiplication again so that's why this matrix representation is very useful to visualize these products so the just do a simple matrix multiplication we see that this then becomes a d cross d matrix right so the outer product essentially or the ket bra gives us matrices right and these matrices will essentially be operators and indeed because it's a linear vector specifically linear operators on the vectors in the vector space right so there are two products that we can consider the inner product which leads us to a number a complex number or a scalar and the outer product which leads us to a matrix or a linear operator right so equipped with this very short recap of linear vector spaces and the bracket notation let us now revisit postulate one right so postulate one says quantum states are vectors in some complex vector space which is equipped with an inner product which means that there is a dual and you can construct the inner product right but what is more it says that there must be unit vectors right the postulate says that these must be unit vectors so what is a unit vector a unit vector is one whose norm is one the norm of the vectors one the norm can be thought of in some sense as the length of a vector if you want to think in terms of real three-dimensional space it is indeed the length of a vector in ah in three-dimensional space right so how do i represent the norm or the length of a vector i simply take the inner product of the vector with itself right so it is nothing but the inner product of psi with itself so for any arbitrary state psi of a quantum system the inner product of psi with itself must be 1 and this is what is called the normalization condition and this this actually is not a mathematical constraint right because all vectors in a vector space need not have unit norm did not have norm one so this actually comes from a physical constraint right and the physical constraint is basically conservation of probabilities so we will soon see how the [Music] uh the components of this vector when you write out the components of this vector those components are essentially probability amplitudes and so they must the square of these probability amplitudes must sum to one right and that's that's the sort of physical constraint that comes

# Week 1: Introduction to Quantum Computing

## Video 4

### Postulates of Quantum Mechanics - I

from the interpretation of these components as probability amplitudes and we will come to that soon when we discuss measurement okay so this is what the postulate said that statement states must be unit vectors in a complex inner product space which we can call a hilbert space because we are dealing with finite dimensions now there are there's one more aspect of this right so the first is the norm right the first is that these must be unit vectors right that was one aspect of it the second is remember that if psi belongs to h then any lambda psi also belongs to h right where lambda is just some number it's just a scalar complex number so it turns out that this lambda that is sitting outside multiplying psi physically does not distinguish the state physically psi and lambda psi correspond to the same state this lambda is not an observable right that can be measured and so the two states psi and lambda side then cannot be distinguished physically they correspond to the same state and so all states quantum states are defined only up to an overall scalar like this right sometimes this overall scalar takes the form of what we call an overall phase right and this overall phase is something which cannot be determined really in quantum theory so all states are only represented up to an overall face right so these are two important aspects over and above the mathematical structure of a linear vector space the fact that the vectors must be unit vectors and second is they are only determined up to an overall scalar so suppose i have a vector which is unnormalized i can use this degree of freedom to normalize the vector all right so let us now look at concrete examples and in particular let us focus our attention on two level quantum systems so two level quantum systems are basically synonymous with qubits so what we are now trying to understand is the state space of qubits so let us first discuss physical examples of this so what is a two level system one of the first things that come to mind so let me call this a1 is polarization states of light so we all know from high school physics that light is a transverse electromagnetic wave so if i have a certain direction of propagation if this is my the y is the direction of propagation then i know that the polarization has to be restricted to a plane that is perpendicular to the direction of propagation so the electric and magnetic fields must oscillate either along the x or along the z direction and an arbitrary polarization vector if we call it p must be constrained to be in the x z plane now this plane of polarization so the plane of polarization is described or characterized by two states right by two perpendicular states namely namely horizontal polarization and vertical polarization so i can associate two orthogonal states or ket vectors with this now so this i can call a sket h this i can call as get v right and my linear vector space associated with the polarization states of light will be constructed as so an arbitrary polarization state if i want to call it p will be represented as some linear combination of h and v so this is an example of a two dimensional quantum system in mathematical terms this represents a two dimensional complex linear vector space and this is denoted as c 2 right and to go back to the qubit association i can associate the horizontal polarized state with get 0 and the vertically polarized state with ket1 and so this constitutes physical realization of a qubit it was indeed one of the first physical earliest physical realizations of a qubit the earliest quantum cryptographic protocols were indeed realized using such polarization states of light right so this is our first example of a two level quantum system there are exactly two distinct states that describe the system mathematically they correspond to two orthogonal states and any other state of the system can be represented as a linear combination of these two states mathematically this

# Week 1: Introduction to Quantum Computing

## Video 4

### Postulates of Quantum Mechanics - I

describes a two dimensional complex linear vector space second example physical example and probably a somewhat more well known physical example especially for the physics students is the spin states of a spin half particle so one can think of the electron or the proton etc so these are all spin half particles right the spin is some intrinsic degree of freedom which can be accessed or probed via an external magnetic field and in the presence of an external magnetic field the electron proton and similar spin half particles right spin states typically these are denoted as spin up and spin down where the up and down are with respect to the direction of an external magnetic field right so this is called a spin up state this is called a spin down state the overall magnitude of the spin is half right so these states are also often called a plus half and a minus half state right these are again two distinct states of a system but of course the electron or the proton or the spin half particles can exist in a linear superposition or combination of up and down spins right so this again to me denotes a two level quantum system with two distinct states namely the spin up state the spin down state or the plus half state and the minus half state so again this is another physical realization of a qubit i can associate these with get 0 and get 1 right and an arbitrary spin state psi can be of the form alpha up plus beta down where again we saw this earlier i mentioned this earlier briefly the alpha and the beta are the probability amplitudes of the state side to exist in either the up spin or the down spin of the electron to exist in either up spin or the spin right so this again defines a two dimensional complex linear vector space so you see for every physical system we construct the associated hilbert space of the linear vector space by considering the distinct states of the system and then taking linear combinations of that gives rise to the vector space in question the third example and the final example that i want to mention is what is called a two level atom and this becomes important in the context of superconducting qubits which is the main architecture that we will be using in the course of this lecture to study quantum circuits and algorithms and so on right the superconducting qubits are essentially artificial two-level atoms the superconducting qubit architecture essentially is made up of artificial two level atoms right what do i mean by two level atom is basically an atomic system which has effectively uh two distinct levels right the system is set up such that it can access these two distinct levels one is a ground state and again i associate a k g with this the other is an excited state and i associate a ket e with this right and all other energy levels are really not accessible energetically not accessible for the system right so essentially there are two levels and these are we can engineer this in atomic systems we can also engineer this on superconducting systems using lc oscillators and such we will not get into the hardware aspects in this lecture but just want to mention that these artificial two level atoms is what forms the backbone of the systems which we will be using in this course and once again we can associate the ground state with our ket zero of the qubit the excited state with the ket one of the qubit and an arbitrary state will be of the form alpha 0 plus beta 1 again these are two distinct orthogonal states this defines for me a two dimensional complex linear vector space so having seen some physical examples and hopefully gain some intuition about what the qubit space looks like let us now formalize the mathematical structure of the qubits state space a little bit more so the qubit state space essentially is comprised of two orthogonal states these are also normalized remember anyway any state of a quantum system has to have unit norm so these are two orthogonal normalized states which

are denoted as 0 and 1 right the fact that they are orthogonal means that the inner product between 0 and 1 this is exactly like the notion of perpendicular vectors in a vector sp in a real vector space right the inner product between 0 and 1 is the same as the inner product between 1 and 0 is 0 the fact that they are normalized means that the inner product of 1 with itself and the inner product of 0 with itself is 1.

- so basically 0 and 1 these two states constitute what is called an orthonormal basis for the qubit state space so mathematically the qubit state space is a two dimensional complex linear vector space we said it comes with an inner product so we can define inner product then we can define orthogonality like this and then we can construct what is called a basis for this two dimensional complex linear vector space the ket 0 and the ket 1 denote the basis states and what is a basis the base is simply is a set of vectors such that any vector in the space can be written as a linear combination of these vectors so indeed for a two dimensional vector space like this we need two orthogonal states and this is what we denote as zero and one and any vector which is which for us is any state of the qubit can be denoted as a complex linear combination of these two basis states right so these are also called the standard basis or the computational basis right so any state can be denoted as a complex linear combination of these two basis states of course states must be unit vectors and therefore the norm of psi must be 1. so this is subject to the constraint the norm of size 1 and i'm sure you can work out using the fact that so let me just write this quickly so norm of psi is simply alpha star 0 plus beta star 1 right times alpha 0 plus beta 1. so we use the fact that 0 and 1 are orthogonal use the fact that they are normalized right so i'm just writing out the brass i and the ket psi and i'm showing you that this inner product is nothing but mod alpha square plus mod beta square so the norm of an arbitrary qubit state is nothing but the modulus squared of alpha and beta and this sum must be equal to 1.

- so this is where the physical description of the qubit comes from furthermore you saw in the earlier segment that we can parametrize psi in terms of two angles right why is that because alpha and beta are complex numbers they can be written in and their complex numbers remember whose modulus squared is one so they can be written as a phase right and i call this phase associated with alpha is e to the i phi alpha times cosine of an angle and beta as a phase times the sine of an angle the sine and the cosine come from the fact that the modulus squared must sum to 1. so that is a reminiscent of sin squared and cos squared summing to 1. so i can parameterize psi in terms of just two angles i know i have written three angles here so theta is an angle which goes from 0 to pi phi is an angle which goes from 0 to 2 pi rather phi alpha and phi beta are both angles that go from 0 to 2 pi now how does this reduce to just two angles from three angles well that happens because remember the state is determined only up to an overall phase so now let me write the state of a qubit in terms of this parameterization right so i can now pull out one of these phases so let me pull out the first phase e to the i phi alpha so that i end up with this description which is cos theta by 2 get 0 sine theta by 2 e to the i phi get 1 right where phi is nothing but phi beta minus phi alpha right so now this is an overall phase which doesn't matter the state is only described up to that so essentially psi is described by simply two angles theta and phi so instead of talking about an arbitrary cubit state in terms of two

complex numbers we can talk about it in terms of two angles which is theta and phi right and so psi can be represented by two angles theta and phi and because you see theta goes from zero to pi and phi goes from zero to two pi this is reminiscent of a sphere in a real three-dimensional space so essentially it can be represented by points on the surface of a sphere in three dimensions this is three real dimensions in three dimensional space and this sphere is what is often called a block poincare sphere right and we saw a pictorial depiction of this in the previous segment where you saw certain specific states marked out at specific points on the surface of this sphere right so let's go ahead and now look at those specific states right so we have looked at two states here first the ket zero and the ket one which constitute one orthonormal basis so let's look at two other states now so so just to recap qubit state space is a two dimensional complex linear vector space with a pair of orthogonal states which we have labeled k zero and ket one but having constructed this space with two basis vectors is 0 1 the only basis for this vector space of course not a vector space admits well infinitely many bases so let us look at some interesting examples of bases which will give us some physical insights right so i would like to construct alternate basis states for c2 right so what are these other basis states that i would like to construct so consider what we define as a ket plus right which is a linear combination of 0 and 1 it is simply 0 plus 1 but 0 plus 1 is not normalized so i need to add a normalization factor which is 1 over root 2 0 plus 1 and i can construct another state get minus which is another superposition of 0 and 1 which is get 0 minus get 1.

- so let me remark at this point that the linearity of the vector space formalism right immediately implies for us in some sense the superposition principle of quantum mechanics and this again is a key point that comes from the structure of the state space namely that any arbitrary linear combination of two states is also a valid state right just want to remark this point here that the linearity of the vector space formalism immediately brings us to the superposition principle of quantum mechanics so plus and minus are two such superposition states that i have created from the zero and the one right of course it is easy for you to check that plus and minus are orthogonal so the inner product between plus and minus which is the same as the inner product between minus and plus zero furthermore they are normalized the inner product of plus with plus and that of minus with minus is one so together this implies that these two states plus and minus also constitute an orthonormal basis for c2 right a third basis so let me call this as basis 2 and then i can construct in fact a third basis which is also relevant which also we marked out on the blocks here in the first lecture so i will call these two states as plus i because now i am going to put a phase in the superposition so i am going to call this make a superposition of the form 0 plus i 1 by root 2 and a minus i state i am going to label this state as minus i again the 1 over root 2 normalization factor exists i'm going to call this make a superposition of the form 0 minus i1 by root 2 right once again one can check that this pair of states is orthogonal these two states are normalized so this also constitutes an orthonormal basis for the qubit state space c2 right so we have now identified three different bases by making appropriate superpositions of the states 0 and 1. how does one actually transform these basis states to get these superpositions so let's try to understand this this is the last point that i want to discuss with regards to the first postulate namely basis transformations so how do we understand basis transformations well basis

transformations are some kind of linear transformations right because remember there is to be some transformation which takes let's say 0 to plus and 1 to minus right so one can think of this as some linear operator right let me call this linear operator as h so h acting on 0 should give me plus which is 0 plus 1 by root 2 and h acting on 1 gives me minus which is 0 minus 1 by root 2 right remember we said that in this bracket formalism these linear transformations are represented by outer products right so one can actually now construct this h by noting these outer products right so we see that h actually must be of the form there must be a one over root two so h actually takes 0 and makes it plus right so this is certainly one term in the outer product expansion because it is taking a ket 0 and mapping it to a plus and then it's taking a ket 1 and mapping it to a minus so i can represent it like this so when i write a bra here this acts on a kit right so when when the first term acts on k 0 you see that the 0 and 0 collapse in the sense that just gives me a 1 that's a number and then i end up with the kit plus similarly when the second term acts on a ket 1 then the 1 and the 1 give me an inner product of 1 and i'm left with the kit minus right so now let's expand the plus and the minus and we get the full matrix representation of this so this is easy to see that this is now of the form 0 0 plus 1 0 plus 0 1 minus 1 1 right so this is in the outer product notation right now if i want to look at the matrix representation so remember we said that these vectors would be represented by column vectors the ket vectors will be represented by column vectors and the bra vectors will be represented by row vectors so then what is the representation of get 0 0 is a vector in a 2 dimensional vector space so the canonical association of the standard association is to associate get 0 with a 2 column vector with entries 1 and 0. and ket 1 is typically associated with a 2 column vector 0 1.

- remember 0 and 1 have to be orthogonal so this is a kind of definition right so we almost define in some sense 0 and 1 to be these column vectors right so this definition is consistent again check that say if i take bra 0 and ket 1 then this inner product remember this is complex conjugate transpose it becomes a row vector 1 0 kept vector 0 1 and of course if i do matrix multiplication this gives me 0. so this is an appropriate definition or an appropriate correspondence for the kept vector and the column vectors the matrix description so if i represent k 0 as 1 0 column vector and ket 1 is a 0 1 column vector then what is get plus right k plus is simply the column vector with an overall 1 over root 2 1 1 and k minus is simply 1 minus 1 right and now when i use this matrix representation and try to write down this operator h then you can see that the operator h actually has the form 1 over root 2 1 1 1 -1 right so this operator h or this linear transformation h we will see in subsequent lectures is actually an important quantum gate called the hadamard gate okay so we stop this module here we basically discussed the mathematical framework for qubit states and discuss the state space of a two level quantum system
